title: Global Event Notifications
summary: Build a global event controller that uses trigger variables for state, presentation effects for player messaging, and optional XS for event selection or calculations.
steps: Create one event-state variable in aoe_variables or equivalent registry.
Create a start trigger gated by timer, area, or variable logic.
Use display_timer for high-frequency visible event messaging or display_instructions for low-frequency major announcements.
Use send_chat only for per-player or low-visibility communication.
Create an end or cleanup trigger that resets the shared event state.
pitfalls: Do not use send_chat as the main global announcement surface when visibility matters.
Do not duplicate event detection logic across many triggers when one shared state variable can control the event.

title: Quest System Flow
summary: Implement quests as modular trigger systems with explicit stage variables, named trigger groups, and separate data/config dictionaries for quest text and thresholds.
steps: Reserve a quest-state variable in the central variable registry.
Create a quest folder with data/config separated from trigger logic.
Split quest logic into offer, accept, progress, completion, and reward phases.
Use trigger conditions like variable_value and objects_in_area to gate progression.
Keep user-facing text and reward values outside the main trigger-building loops.
pitfalls: Do not hide quest stages in scattered unnamed variables.
Do not mix quest text, balancing, and trigger lifecycle logic into one long file.

title: Teleport System
summary: Use parser-manager placement for teleport objects, trigger variables for teleport state, and XS only when the runtime behavior needs more complex computation than triggers should own.
steps: Place teleport objects or markers using parser-manager setup code.
Create trigger-side activation logic around object interaction or area checks.
Track shared teleport state with trigger variables when multiple systems depend on it.
Use XS only for calculations or repeated checks that are awkward in pure triggers.
Keep teleport placement, teleport rules, and teleport visuals in separate modules.
pitfalls: Do not bury teleport placement and teleport runtime logic in one monolithic file.
Do not promote XS to the owner of the whole system when triggers can own the event flow.

title: Loot And Pickup System
summary: Model loot as placed or converted scenario objects plus trigger-based pickup resolution, with balance values stored separately and optional data-driven item mappings.
steps: Identify or create the loot objects in the scenario or dat layer.
Store bonus values in data dictionaries or JSON rather than hardcoding them repeatedly.
Create pickup triggers gated by area, object state, or ownership rules.
Resolve the reward through shared variable updates, resource effects, or stat effects.
Keep naming and visual identity setup separate from the reward logic.
pitfalls: Do not mix loot visual setup and reward logic into one brittle function.
Do not bury item-to-reward mappings inside trigger loops when a data file can hold them.

title: Class Pick And Progression
summary: Implement class picking and progression as layered systems: selection/setup, variable ownership, ability gating, and balance dictionaries for stats and spell values.
steps: Allocate class and progression variables centrally.
Separate class definition data from class-trigger logic.
Use feature folders per class or progression domain.
Gate abilities and progression through shared variables rather than ad hoc checks.
Use dat-side support only for base units, UI units, graphics, or asset-linked class content.
pitfalls: Do not spread class-state ownership across unrelated modules.
Do not couple progression balance values directly to trigger implementation code.

title: NPC Names And Roles
summary: Use parser-manager access to placed units, then apply modular trigger systems or helper managers for names, professions, or NPC role logic.
steps: Collect or filter target units through parser-manager code.
Keep naming and profession data in separate registries or helper systems.
Apply role-specific naming or state rules through a dedicated system module.
Keep NPC identity initialization separate from quests or loot logic.
pitfalls: Do not mix NPC naming, quests, and combat logic into one setup pass.
Do not hardcode repeated profession/name tables inside leaf trigger builders.

title: Terrain-Driven Map Setup
summary: Use MapManager to scan terrain or regions, then place blockers, markers, or world objects through UnitManager before gameplay triggers take over.
steps: Read terrain or regions through MapManager.
Decide which tiles or rectangles should receive world objects.
Place blockers, markers, or setup objects with UnitManager.
Keep the terrain-scan pass separate from runtime quest or combat systems.
pitfalls: Do not run heavy terrain-scan logic in gameplay trigger modules when one setup pass is enough.
Do not confuse parser-manager setup with runtime event handling.

title: Revealers And Visibility Systems
summary: Combine parser-manager unit placement with trigger-side ownership and activation logic to control map vision, revealers, and visibility-based systems.
steps: Place revealer units in scenario setup where visibility is needed.
Assign correct player ownership and visibility intent at placement time.
Use triggers to activate, freeze, or otherwise control related gameplay state.
Keep the visual placement layer separate from event-driven reveal logic.
pitfalls: Do not treat revealer placement as a pure trigger problem when parser-manager setup is simpler.
Do not hide revealer ownership assumptions inside unrelated world systems.

title: Shop System Flow
summary: Build shops as modular NPC or building interactions with item data separated from trigger logic, shared currency variables, and explicit purchase-validation triggers.
steps: Store shop inventory, prices, and reward mappings in a dedicated data dictionary instead of scattering them across triggers.
Create one interaction trigger group for opening or selecting the shop entrypoint.
Validate purchase conditions through currency variables, inventory limits, or class restrictions before granting the item.
Apply the purchased reward through item-grant triggers, stat effects, or linked dat-backed items.
Keep shop presentation text and balancing data separate from the trigger-building code.
pitfalls: Do not hardcode prices and rewards in long trigger blocks when a data layer can hold them.
Do not mix shop interaction, affordability checks, and reward application into one monolithic trigger.

title: NPC Interaction And Dialogue
summary: Treat NPC interaction as a reusable trigger system with separate dialogue data, interaction-state variables, and optional branching based on quest or class state.
steps: Reserve trigger variables for interaction state, current dialogue branch, or one-time completion flags.
Store dialogue text and branch metadata in a data file or dictionary rather than embedding every line in trigger construction code.
Build one reusable interaction pattern for approach, select, confirm, and exit behavior.
Gate dialogue branches with quest-stage, class, or resource conditions instead of duplicating near-identical trigger trees.
Keep follow-up actions such as rewards, teleports, or shop opens in separate modules that the dialogue system calls.
pitfalls: Do not mix dialogue text storage with trigger orchestration when you expect later writing or balancing passes.
Do not duplicate one-off NPC interaction code when the same trigger pattern can be parameterized.

title: Class Ability Activation
summary: Implement class abilities as data-driven systems with separated cooldown or cost data, trigger-owned activation flow, and XS support only when targeting or calculations become too complex for pure triggers.
steps: Store cooldown, cost, and balance values in a dedicated data module or dictionary for the class system.
Build one activation flow that checks ownership, class state, cost, and cooldown before firing the ability.
Split the system into activate, resolve, cooldown, and cleanup phases rather than keeping the whole ability in one block.
Use XS only when target scans, repeated calculations, or runtime loops are awkward in pure triggers.
Keep visuals, gameplay effect application, and balancing data in separate modules so later balance passes are cheap.
pitfalls: Do not hardcode cooldowns and costs across several modules when the class data layer should own them.
Do not push simple trigger-owned activation logic into XS unless the computation actually needs it.

title: Boss Encounter Flow
summary: Treat boss fights as staged encounter controllers with encounter-state variables, separate phase data, area start logic, and isolated reward or cleanup modules.
steps: Reserve encounter-state and phase variables before building the boss triggers.
Split the boss system into encounter start, active phases, transitions, fail/reset, and reward phases.
Store phase thresholds, wave definitions, and rewards in data dictionaries so the fight can be balanced without rewriting logic.
Use trigger area checks and boss-state checks for phase transitions, and call XS only when repeated scans or target selection become complex.
Keep arena setup, boss stats, summons, and reward payout in separate modules owned by the encounter controller.
pitfalls: Do not scatter phase logic across unrelated combat files with no single encounter owner.
Do not embed phase thresholds and reward values directly into trigger-building loops when they need balancing later.

title: Summon And Spawn System
summary: Model summons as explicit spawn workflows with source ownership, duration or cleanup variables, and separate data for spawned units, limits, and follow-up behavior.
steps: Store summon unit ids, durations, and per-summon limits in a data dictionary rather than hardcoding them into trigger logic.
Split the flow into cast or activation, spawn placement, active lifetime, and cleanup phases.
Use trigger variables to track summon ownership, active counts, or expiration state.
Use XS only when repeated target scans or spawn-location calculations are awkward in pure triggers.
Keep summon visuals, summon unit data, and summon cleanup rules in separate modules.
pitfalls: Do not mix summon creation, duration tracking, and cleanup in one oversized trigger block.
Do not hide spawn-unit mappings inside code when they belong in data.

title: Wave System Controller
summary: Build wave gameplay as a controller with wave-state variables, data-driven wave definitions, and separate spawn, progress, and cleanup modules.
steps: Reserve central variables for current wave, active-wave state, and completion flags.
Store wave unit compositions, spawn timing, and rewards in a separate data table or dictionary.
Split the system into wave start, spawn execution, wave completion detection, and next-wave transition logic.
Use XS only when the wave controller needs repeated scans, weighted selection, or more complex runtime calculations.
Keep wave definitions and balancing data out of the trigger-construction loops.
pitfalls: Do not hardcode each wave in a separate unstructured trigger tree when a wave data table can drive the controller.
Do not mix reward payout and wave-spawn logic into one phase.

title: Reward Table Resolution
summary: Treat rewards as data-driven tables that map events, quest stages, drops, or achievements to payout actions so balancing changes stay cheap.
steps: Move reward values and item or currency mappings into a dedicated reward table or dictionary file.
Use one trigger-side reward resolver that reads the reward type and dispatches the correct payout behavior.
Gate reward resolution with eligibility variables so one-time rewards and repeatable rewards stay explicit.
Keep reward payout separate from the event logic that caused the reward so later balance passes only touch the data layer.
Use dat-backed items or units only through dedicated reward handlers rather than inline mutation logic.
pitfalls: Do not duplicate reward amounts across several systems when one table should own them.
Do not combine eligibility checks and payout mutations in ways that make reward balancing hard to audit.

title: Fail And Reset Flow
summary: Implement fail states and resets as a dedicated controller with checkpoint data, cleanup triggers, and restore steps so encounters can restart cleanly without rebuilding the whole scenario state by hand.
steps: Reserve shared variables for current checkpoint, fail state, and reset-in-progress state.
Keep checkpoint data such as restored spawns, resources, or quest-stage values in a dedicated data dictionary.
Create one fail-detection trigger group that only decides when a reset should begin.
Create a reset controller that performs cleanup, restores checkpoint data, and re-enables the encounter in a fixed order.
Keep player-facing fail or retry messaging separate from the cleanup and restore operations.
pitfalls: Do not mix fail detection, cleanup, and checkpoint restore logic into one giant trigger.
Do not hardcode checkpoint values in reset code when the encounter needs balancing or multiple reset points later.

title: Class Stat Scaling Tables
summary: Drive class progression from separate scaling tables or dictionaries so triggers and XS apply level-based stat changes without hardcoding every value in the implementation layer.
steps: Store per-class stat growth in data dictionaries or JSON keyed by class and level rather than in trigger branches.
Let one progression or stat-scaling controller read the current class and level state from shared variables.
Apply the resolved values through trigger effects, XS stat helpers, or dat-backed support assets depending on the stat type.
Keep scaling-table ownership separate from class-pick flow and combat-resolution flow.
When balancing changes happen, update the data layer first and avoid editing the core trigger orchestration.
pitfalls: Do not hardcode every class or level branch inside trigger construction code when the values belong in data.
Do not let multiple systems own the same final stat calculation path without one scaling controller.

title: Boss Reward Distribution
summary: Resolve boss rewards through a dedicated payout layer that runs after encounter completion so loot, currencies, unlocks, and follow-up progression all come from one reusable reward pipeline.
steps: Keep boss completion detection separate from the reward payout controller.
Resolve the reward payload by boss id, difficulty, or encounter stage through a shared reward-table layer.
Apply currencies, loot, unlocks, or progression changes through small payout modules rather than one long reward trigger.
Track first-clear or repeat-clear state in dedicated variables when payouts differ by attempt.
Keep boss reward data independent from boss combat tuning so balance passes do not force trigger rewrites.
pitfalls: Do not embed reward payout rules directly inside the boss combat state machine.
Do not duplicate the same payout logic across bosses when one shared reward pipeline can parameterize it.
